narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, you've put together a respectable academic profile, including middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students in this dataset. However, the university's machine learning model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 56.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your record that stood out as the strongest influences. The most significant factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who tend to pass the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed in the same direction, though with less force, again highlighting early grades that lagged behind the dataset average of about 5.7 and correlating with higher failure risk in similar students. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually pulled the prediction toward success, as its near-average value of 3.20 (matching the typical 3.21) is a positive signal associated with bar passers. Overall, these academic deciles outweighed your other strengths, painting a picture riskier than most applicants whose rankings align closer to the middle of their classes. While your LSAT and family background played supporting roles without major sway here, the model saw your position relative to typical students—who often rank higher in school deciles—as the tipping point for denial. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven caution based on patterns from thousands of past cases. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted GPA improvement, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing exactly these gaps, so focus there for your next steps. With persistence, you can shift the odds in your favor.
